   147→// checkAllSourcesReady returns true if every source in the project has a
   148→// release matching the target version.
   149→func (o *Orchestrator) checkAllSourcesReady(ctx context.Context, projectID, version string) (bool, error) {
   150→	// Use a high limit to fetch all sources; projects rarely exceed a handful.
   151→	sources, _, err := o.store.ListSourcesByProject(ctx, projectID, 1, 1000)
   152→	if err != nil {
   153→		return false, fmt.Errorf("list sources: %w", err)
   154→	}
   155→	if len(sources) == 0 {
   156→		return true, nil
   157→	}
   158→
   159→	for _, src := range sources {
   160→		has, err := o.store.HasReleaseForVersion(ctx, src.ID, version)
   161→		if err != nil {
   162→			return false, fmt.Errorf("check source %s: %w", src.ID, err)
   163→		}
   164→		if !has {
   165→			slog.Info("agent: source not ready for version",
   166→				"project_id", projectID,
   167→				"source_id", src.ID,
   168→				"version", version,
   169→			)
   170→			return false, nil
   171→		}
   172→	}
   173→	return true, nil
   174→}
   175→
   176→// Orchestrator manages the lifecycle of an agent run: loading project config,
   177→// creating an ADK-Go agent with project-specific tools and instructions,
   178→// running the agent, and persisting the result as a semantic release.
   179→type Orchestrator struct {
   180→	store     OrchestratorStore
   181→	llmConfig LLMConfig
   182→}
   183→
   184→// NewOrchestrator creates a new Orchestrator. It validates that the provided
   185→// LLMConfig has the required API key for the configured provider. If the key
   186→// is missing, it returns an error so the caller can degrade gracefully.
   187→func NewOrchestrator(store OrchestratorStore, cfg LLMConfig) (*Orchestrator, error) {
   188→	switch cfg.Provider {
   189→	case "openai":
   190→		if cfg.OpenAIAPIKey == "" {
   191→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("OPENAI_API_KEY is not set; agent orchestrator requires an LLM API key")
   192→		}
   193→	default: // gemini
   194→		if cfg.GoogleAPIKey == "" {
   195→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("GOOGLE_API_KEY is not set; agent orchestrator requires an LLM API key")
   196→		}
   197→	}
   198→	return &Orchestrator{
   199→		store:     store,
   200→		llmConfig: cfg,
   201→	}, nil
   202→}
   203→
   204→// RunAgent executes the full agent lifecycle for a given agent run:
   205→//  1. Marks the run as "running".
   206→//  2. Loads the project and builds the agent prompt.
   207→//  3. Creates an ADK-Go LLM agent with project-scoped tools.
   208→//  4. Runs the agent and captures its final text response.
   209→//  5. Parses the response into a SemanticReport.
   210→//  6. Creates a SemanticRelease and links it to the agent run.
   211→//  7. Marks the run as "completed" (or "failed" on error).
   212→func (o *Orchestrator) RunAgent(ctx context.Context, run *models.AgentRun) error {
   213→	// Mark as running.
   214→	if err := o.store.UpdateAgentRunStatus(ctx, run.ID, "running"); err != nil {
   215→		return fmt.Errorf("update agent run status to running: %w", err)
   216→	}
   217→
   218→	// Execute the agent; capture any error to mark the run as failed.
   219→	result, err := o.executeAgent(ctx, run)
   220→	if err != nil {
   221→		slog.Error("agent run failed", "run_id", run.ID, "err", err)
   222→		// Best-effort: mark the run as failed.
   223→		if statusErr := o.store.UpdateAgentRunStatus(ctx, run.ID, "failed"); statusErr != nil {
   224→			slog.Error("agent: failed to mark run as failed",
   225→				"run_id", run.ID, "status_err", statusErr)
   226→		}
   227→		return err
   228→	}
   229→
   230→	// Link the semantic release to the agent run and mark completed.
   231→	if err := o.store.UpdateAgentRunResult(ctx, run.ID, result.semanticReleaseID); err != nil {
   232→		return fmt.Errorf("update agent run result: %w", err)
   233→	}
   234→	if err := o.store.UpdateAgentRunStatus(ctx, run.ID, "completed"); err != nil {
   235→		return fmt.Errorf("update agent run status to completed: %w", err)
   236→	}
   237→
   238→	// Send project-level notifications (best-effort; errors are logged, not returned).
   239→	o.sendProjectNotifications(ctx, run, result)
   240→
   241→	return nil
   242→}
   243→
   244→// agentResult holds the output of a successful agent execution, used to
   245→// construct notifications after the semantic release is persisted.
   246→type agentResult struct {
   247→	semanticReleaseID string
   248→	version           string
   249→	reportText        string
   250→	projectName       string
   251→}
   252→
   253→// executeAgent performs the actual LLM agent interaction and returns the
   254→// agentResult containing the semantic release ID and metadata for notifications.
   255→func (o *Orchestrator) executeAgent(ctx context.Context, run *models.AgentRun) (*agentResult, error) {
   256→	// Load project.
   257→	slog.Info("agent: loading project", "run_id", run.ID, "project_id", run.ProjectID)
   258→	project, err := o.store.GetProject(ctx, run.ProjectID)
   259→	if err != nil {
   260→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("get project: %w", err)
   261→	}
   262→	slog.Info("agent: project loaded", "run_id", run.ID, "project", project.Name)
   263→
   264→	// Extract target version from the agent run.
   265→	version := run.Version
   266→	if version == "" {
   267→		// Fallback: parse from trigger "auto:version:v1.10.15"
   268→		if strings.HasPrefix(run.Trigger, "auto:version:") {
   269→			version = strings.TrimPrefix(run.Trigger, "auto:version:")
   270→		}
   271→	}
   272→
   273→	// Build the agent using the shared constructor.
   274→	slog.Info("agent: building agent", "run_id", run.ID,
   275→		"provider", o.llmConfig.Provider, "model", o.llmConfig.Model,
   276→		"version", version)
   277→	agentInstance, err := BuildAgent(ctx, o.store, project, o.llmConfig, version)
   278→	if err != nil {
   279→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("build agent: %w", err)
   280→	}
   281→
   282→	// Create in-memory session service and a new session.
   283→	sessionService := session.InMemoryService()
   284→	createResp, err := sessionService.Create(ctx, &session.CreateRequest{
   285→		AppName: "changelogue",
   286→		UserID:  "system",
   287→	})
   288→	if err != nil {
   289→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("create session: %w", err)
   290→	}
   291→	sess := createResp.Session
   292→
   293→	// Create the runner with logging plugin for agent trace visibility.
   294→	r, err := runner.New(runner.Config{
   295→		AppName:        "changelogue",
   296→		Agent:          agentInstance,
   297→		SessionService: sessionService,
   298→		PluginConfig: runner.PluginConfig{
   299→			Plugins:      []*plugin.Plugin{loggingplugin.MustNew("agent_trace")},
   300→			CloseTimeout: 5 * time.Second,
   301→		},
   302→	})
   303→	if err != nil {
   304→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("create runner: %w", err)
   305→	}
   306→
   307→	// Run the agent with a prompt requesting analysis.
   308→	slog.Info("agent: starting LLM run", "run_id", run.ID, "project", project.Name, "version", version)
   309→	var userPrompt string
   310→	if version != "" {
   311→		userPrompt = fmt.Sprintf("Analyze version %s for this project. Cross-check all sources and produce a semantic release report.", version)
   312→	} else {
   313→		userPrompt = "Analyze the recent releases for this project and produce a semantic release report."
   314→	}
   315→	userMsg := genai.NewContentFromText(userPrompt, "user")
   316→
   317→	var finalText string
   318→	eventCount := 0
   319→	for event, err := range r.Run(ctx, "system", sess.ID(), userMsg, agent.RunConfig{}) {
   320→		if err != nil {
   321→			slog.Error("agent: run event error",
   322→				"run_id", run.ID,
   323→				"event_count", eventCount,
   324→				"err", err,
   325→			)
   326→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("agent run event error: %w", err)
   327→		}
   328→		eventCount++
   329→		if event != nil && event.IsFinalResponse() && event.Content != nil {
   330→			for _, part := range event.Content.Parts {
   331→				if part.Text != "" {
   332→					finalText += part.Text
   333→				}
   334→			}
   335→		}
   336→	}
   337→	slog.Info("agent: LLM run finished",
   338→		"run_id", run.ID,
   339→		"event_count", eventCount,
   340→		"output_length", len(finalText),
   341→	)
   342→
   343→	if finalText == "" {
   344→		slog.Error("agent: produced no output",
   345→			"run_id", run.ID,
   346→			"project_id", run.ProjectID,
   347→			"event_count", eventCount,
   348→		)
   349→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("agent produced no output")
   350→	}
   351→
   352→	// Parse the agent's response into a SemanticReport.
   353→	report, err := parseReport(finalText)
   354→	if err != nil {
   355→		slog.Warn("agent output was not valid JSON report, storing raw",
   356→			"run_id", run.ID, "parse_err", err, "raw_output", finalText)
   357→		// Fall back to storing the raw text as the summary.
   358→		report = &models.SemanticReport{
   359→			Summary: finalText,
   360→		}
   361→	}
   362→
   363→	reportJSON, err := json.Marshal(report)
   364→	if err != nil {
   365→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("marshal report: %w", err)
   366→	}
   367→
   368→	// Gather release IDs for the semantic_release_sources join table.
   369→	releases, _, err := o.store.ListReleasesByProject(ctx, run.ProjectID, 1, 50)
   370→	if err != nil {
   371→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("list releases for semantic release: %w", err)
   372→	}
   373→	releaseIDs := make([]string, 0, len(releases))
   374→	for _, r := range releases {
   375→		releaseIDs = append(releaseIDs, r.ID)
   376→	}
   377→
   378→	// Use the target version for the semantic release.
   379→	srVersion := version
   380→	if srVersion == "" {
   381→		srVersion = "unknown"
   382→		if len(releases) > 0 {
   383→			srVersion = releases[0].Version
   384→		}
   385→	}
   386→
   387→	now := time.Now()
   388→	sr := &models.SemanticRelease{
   389→		ProjectID:   run.ProjectID,
   390→		Version:     srVersion,
   391→		Report:      reportJSON,
   392→		Status:      "completed",
   393→		CompletedAt: &now,
   394→	}
   395→
   396→	if err := o.store.CreateSemanticRelease(ctx, sr, releaseIDs); err != nil {
   397→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("create semantic release: %w", err)
   398→	}
   399→
   400→	slog.Info("agent run produced semantic release",
   401→		"run_id", run.ID,
   402→		"semantic_release_id", sr.ID,
   403→		"version", sr.Version,
   404→	)
   405→
   406→	return &agentResult{
   407→		semanticReleaseID: sr.ID,
   408→		version:           srVersion,
   409→		reportText:        finalText,
   410→		projectName:       project.Name,
   411→	}, nil
   412→}
   413→
   414→// defaultSenders returns a map of channel type to Sender for the supported
   415→// notification channel types (webhook, slack, discord).
   416→func defaultSenders() map[string]routing.Sender {
   417→	httpClient := &http.Client{Timeout: 10 * time.Second}
   418→	return map[string]routing.Sender{
   419→		"webhook": &routing.WebhookSender{Client: httpClient},
   420→		"slack":   &routing.SlackSender{Client: httpClient},
   421→		"discord": &routing.DiscordSender{Client: httpClient},
   422→	}
   423→}
   424→
   425→// sendProjectNotifications looks up project-level subscriptions and sends a
   426→// notification to each channel with the semantic release report. This is
   427→// best-effort: errors are logged but do not propagate to the caller.
   428→func (o *Orchestrator) sendProjectNotifications(ctx context.Context, run *models.AgentRun, result *agentResult) {
   429→	subs, err := o.store.ListProjectSubscriptions(ctx, run.ProjectID)
   430→	if err != nil {
   431→		slog.Error("list project subscriptions for notification",
   432→			"project_id", run.ProjectID, "err", err)
   433→		return
   434→	}
   435→	if len(subs) == 0 {
   436→		slog.Debug("agent: no project subscriptions configured",
   437→			"project_id", run.ProjectID)
   438→		return
   439→	}
   440→
   441→	senders := defaultSenders()
   442→
   443→	msg := routing.Notification{
   444→		Title:   fmt.Sprintf("Semantic Release Report: %s %s", result.projectName, result.version),
   445→		Body:    result.reportText,
   446→		Version: result.version,
   447→	}
   448→
   449→	for _, sub := range subs {
   450→		ch, err := o.store.GetChannel(ctx, sub.ChannelID)
   451→		if err != nil {
   452→			slog.Error("get channel for project notification",
   453→				"channel_id", sub.ChannelID, "err", err)
   454→			continue
   455→		}
   456→
   457→		sender, ok := senders[ch.Type]
   458→		if !ok {
   459→			slog.Warn("unknown channel type for project notification", "type", ch.Type)
   460→			continue
   461→		}
   462→
   463→		if err := sender.Send(ctx, ch, msg); err != nil {
   464→			slog.Error("send project notification failed",
   465→				"channel", ch.Name, "type", ch.Type, "err", err)
   466→		} else {
   467→			slog.Info("project notification sent",
   468→				"channel", ch.Name, "project_id", run.ProjectID, "version", result.version)
   469→		}
   470→	}
   471→}
   472→
   473→// parseReport attempts to parse the agent's text output as a SemanticReport JSON.
   474→// It handles cases where the agent wraps the JSON in markdown code blocks.
   475→func parseReport(text string) (*models.SemanticReport, error) {
   476→	// Strip markdown code fences if present.
   477→	cleaned := strings.TrimSpace(text)
   478→	if strings.HasPrefix(cleaned, "```") {
   479→		// Remove opening fence (with optional language tag).
   480→		if idx := strings.Index(cleaned, "\n"); idx != -1 {
   481→			cleaned = cleaned[idx+1:]
   482→		}
   483→		// Remove closing fence.
   484→		if idx := strings.LastIndex(cleaned, "```"); idx != -1 {
   485→			cleaned = cleaned[:idx]
   486→		}
   487→		cleaned = strings.TrimSpace(cleaned)
   488→	}
   489→
   490→	var report models.SemanticReport
   491→	if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(cleaned), &report); err != nil {
   492→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("parse report JSON: %w", err)
   493→	}
   494→
   495→	if report.Subject == "" && report.Summary == "" {
   496→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("report is missing required 'subject' or 'summary' field")
   497→	}
   498→
   499→	return &report, nil
   500→}
   501→

content.text: Interesting — OpenAI's web search works differently from what I expected. It's a model-level option (`web_search_options`), not a tool in the `tools` array. This has implications for the design. Let me also check if there's a Responses API approach.

input.query: Responses API web_search tool type format, how to use web search as a tool in the responses API

content.text:
### Enable Web Search Tool in OpenAI Responses API

Source: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/tools-web-search_api-mode=responses

This snippet demonstrates how to configure the OpenAI Responses API to utilize the `web_search` tool. By including `{"type": "web_search"}` within the `tools` array of the API request, the model gains the ability to perform web searches to answer the provided input prompt. This functionality allows models to access current information from the internet, enhancing the relevance and accuracy of their responses.

```javascript
import OpenAI from "openai";
const client = new OpenAI();

const response = await client.responses.create({
    model: "gpt-5",
    tools: [
        { type: "web_search" }
    ],
    input: "What was a positive news story from today?"
});

console.log(response.output_text);
```

```python
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

response = client.responses.create(
    model="gpt-5",
    tools=[{"type": "web_search"}],
    input="What was a positive news story from today?"
)

print(response.output_text);
```

```curl
curl "https://api.openai.com/v1/responses" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY" \
    -d '{
        "model": "gpt-5",
        "tools": [{"type": "web_search"}],
        "input": "what was a positive news story from today?"
    }'
```

```csharp
using OpenAI.Responses;

string key = Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable("OPENAI_API_KEY")!;
OpenAIResponseClient client = new(model: "gpt-5", apiKey: key);

ResponseCreationOptions options = new();
options.Tools.Add(ResponseTool.CreateWebSearchTool());

OpenAIResponse response = (OpenAIResponse)client.CreateResponse([
    ResponseItem.CreateUserMessageItem([
        ResponseContentPart.CreateInputTextPart("What was a positive news story from today?")
    ])
], options);

Console.WriteLine(response.GetOutputText());
```

--------------------------------

### POST /responses - Web Search Tool (Native)

Source: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/responses-vs-chat-completions

Create a response using the newer Responses API with native web search tool support. This simplified endpoint uses OpenAI's native tools without requiring custom function definitions.

```APIDOC
## POST /responses

### Description
Create a response using the Responses API with native web search tool integration. This is the recommended approach for new applications, providing simplified tool configuration and improved reasoning capabilities.

### Method
POST

### Endpoint
/responses

### Parameters
#### Request Body
- **model** (string) - Required - The model identifier (e.g., 'gpt-5')
- **input** (string) - Required - The user input or question
- **tools** (array) - Optional - Array of tool objects to enable
  - **type** (string) - Tool type (e.g., 'web_search')

### Request Example
```javascript
const answer = await client.responses.create({
  model: 'gpt-5',
  input: 'Who is the current president of France?',
  tools: [{ type: 'web_search' }]
});

console.log(answer.output_text);
```

### Response
#### Success Response (200)
- **id** (string) - Unique response identifier
- **output_text** (string) - The generated response text
- **model** (string) - Model used for generation
- **usage** (object) - Token usage information
  - **input_tokens** (integer) - Tokens used in input
  - **output_tokens** (integer) - Tokens used in output

### Response Example
```json
{
  "id": "resp-123",
  "output_text": "The current president of France is Emmanuel Macron. He has been in office since May 2017 and was re-elected in 2022.",
  "model": "gpt-5",
  "usage": {
    "input_tokens": 15,
    "output_tokens": 35
  }
}
```
```

--------------------------------

### POST /v1/responses - Web Search Tool

Source: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/tools-web-search_api-mode=responses

Create a response with web search enabled using the Responses API. The model can choose to search the web based on the input prompt. This endpoint supports three types of web search: non-reasoning for quick lookups, agentic search with reasoning models for complex workflows, and deep research for extended investigations.

```APIDOC
## POST /v1/responses

### Description
Create a response with web search capability enabled. The model will have access to web search tools and can choose to search the web based on the input prompt to provide up-to-date information with sourced citations.

### Method
POST

### Endpoint
https://api.openai.com/v1/responses

### Parameters
#### Request Body
- **model** (string) - Required - The model to use (e.g., "gpt-5", "o3-deep-research", "o4-mini-deep-research")
- **tools** (array) - Required - Array of tools to enable. Include {"type": "web_search"} to enable web search
- **input** (string) - Required - The user's query or prompt for the model

### Request Example
```json
{
  "model": "gpt-5",
  "tools": [
    {"type": "web_search"}
  ],
  "input": "What was a positive news story from today?"
}
```

### Response
#### Success Response (200)
- **output_text** (string) - The model's response based on web search results with sourced citations
- **model** (string) - The model used for the response
- **tools_used** (array) - Array of tools that were utilized by the model

#### Response Example
```json
{
  "output_text": "[Model response with web search results and citations]",
  "model": "gpt-5",
  "tools_used": ["web_search"]
}
```

### Web Search Types
1. **Non-reasoning web search** - Fast method for quick lookups. Model sends query to web search tool and returns top results without internal planning.
2. **Agentic search with reasoning models** - Model actively manages search process, analyzes results, and decides whether to continue searching. Better for complex workflows.
3. **Deep research** - Specialized agent-driven method for in-depth investigations. Can tap into hundreds of sources and run for several minutes. Best used with background mode.

### Code Examples

#### JavaScript
```javascript
import OpenAI from "openai";
const client = new OpenAI();

const response = await client.responses.create({
    model: "gpt-5",
    tools: [
        { type: "web_search" },
    ],
    input: "What was a positive news story from today?",
});

console.log(response.output_text);
```

#### Python
```python
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

response = client.responses.create(
    model="gpt-5",
    tools=[{"type": "web_search"}],
    input="What was a positive news story from today?"
)

print(response.output_text)
```

#### cURL
```bash
curl "https://api.openai.com/v1/responses" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY" \
    -d '{
        "model": "gpt-5",
        "tools": [{"type": "web_search"}],
        "input": "what was a positive news story from today?"
    }'
```

#### C#
```csharp
using OpenAI.Responses;

string key = Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable("OPENAI_API_KEY")!;
OpenAIResponseClient client = new(model: "gpt-5", apiKey: key);

ResponseCreationOptions options = new();
options.Tools.Add(ResponseTool.CreateWebSearchTool());

OpenAIResponse response = (OpenAIResponse)client.CreateResponse([
    ResponseItem.CreateUserMessageItem([
        ResponseContentPart.CreateInputTextPart("What was a positive news story from today?"),
    ]),
], options);

Console.WriteLine(response.GetOutputText());
```
```

--------------------------------

### POST /v1/responses (Web Search)

Source: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/tools

This endpoint allows you to create a model response that includes web search results. By specifying "web_search" as a tool, the model can access up-to-date information from the internet to inform its output.

```APIDOC
## POST /v1/responses

### Description
This endpoint allows you to create a model response that includes web search results. By specifying "web_search" as a tool, the model can access up-to-date information from the internet to inform its output.

### Method
POST

### Endpoint
/v1/responses

### Parameters
#### Path Parameters
(None)

#### Query Parameters
(None)

#### Request Body
- **model** (string) - Required - The ID of the model to use.
- **tools** (array of objects) - Required - A list of tools the model may use. For web search, include an object with `type: "web_search"`.
- **input** (string or array of objects) - Required - The user's input message.

### Request Example
{
  "model": "gpt-5",
  "tools": [{"type": "web_search"}],
  "input": "what was a positive news story from today?"
}

### Response
#### Success Response (200)
- **output_text** (string) - The model's response, potentially incorporating web search results.

#### Response Example
{
  "output_text": "A positive news story from today is that scientists have made a breakthrough in..."
}
```

--------------------------------

### POST /v1/responses - Web Search Tool

Source: https://platform.openai.com/docs/quickstart_api-mode=responses

This endpoint allows you to use the OpenAI model with a built-in web search tool. The model can then access external information from the web to formulate its response.

```APIDOC
## POST /v1/responses

### Description
This endpoint allows you to use the OpenAI model with a built-in web search tool. The model can then access external information from the web to formulate its response.

### Method
POST

### Endpoint
/v1/responses

### Parameters
#### Path Parameters
(None)

#### Query Parameters
(None)

#### Request Body
- **model** (string) - Required - The ID of the model to use (e.g., "gpt-5").
- **input** (string) - Required - The input text or prompt for the model.
- **tools** (array of objects) - Required - A list of tools to enable for the model.
  - Each tool object:
    - **type** (string) - Required - The type of the tool. For web search, use "web_search".

### Request Example
```json
{
  "model": "gpt-5",
  "tools": [
    {
      "type": "web_search"
    }
  ],
  "input": "What was a positive news story from today?"
}
```

### Response
#### Success Response (200)
- **output_text** (string) - The model's generated response, potentially incorporating information from web search.

#### Response Example
```json
{
  "output_text": "A positive news story from today is that scientists have developed a new biodegradable plastic alternative made from seaweed, which could significantly reduce ocean pollution."
}
```
```

### Enable Web Search Tool in OpenAI Responses API

Source: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/tools-web-search_api-mode=responses

This snippet demonstrates how to configure the OpenAI Responses API to utilize the `web_search` tool. By including `{"type": "web_search"}` within the `tools` array of the API request, the model gains the ability to perform web searches to answer the provided input prompt. This functionality allows models to access current information from the internet, enhancing the relevance and accuracy of their responses.

```javascript
import OpenAI from "openai";
const client = new OpenAI();

const response = await client.responses.create({
    model: "gpt-5",
    tools: [
        { type: "web_search" }
    ],
    input: "What was a positive news story from today?"
});

console.log(response.output_text);
```

```python
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

response = client.responses.create(
    model="gpt-5",
    tools=[{"type": "web_search"}],
    input="What was a positive news story from today?"
)

print(response.output_text);
```

```curl
curl "https://api.openai.com/v1/responses" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY" \
    -d '{
        "model": "gpt-5",
        "tools": [{"type": "web_search"}],
        "input": "what was a positive news story from today?"
    }'
```

```csharp
using OpenAI.Responses;

string key = Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable("OPENAI_API_KEY")!;
OpenAIResponseClient client = new(model: "gpt-5", apiKey: key);

ResponseCreationOptions options = new();
options.Tools.Add(ResponseTool.CreateWebSearchTool());

OpenAIResponse response = (OpenAIResponse)client.CreateResponse([
    ResponseItem.CreateUserMessageItem([
        ResponseContentPart.CreateInputTextPart("What was a positive news story from today?")
    ])
], options);

Console.WriteLine(response.GetOutputText());
```

--------------------------------

### POST /responses - Web Search Tool (Native)

Source: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/responses-vs-chat-completions

Create a response using the newer Responses API with native web search tool support. This simplified endpoint uses OpenAI's native tools without requiring custom function definitions.

```APIDOC
## POST /responses

### Description
Create a response using the Responses API with native web search tool integration. This is the recommended approach for new applications, providing simplified tool configuration and improved reasoning capabilities.

### Method
POST

### Endpoint
/responses

### Parameters
#### Request Body
- **model** (string) - Required - The model identifier (e.g., 'gpt-5')
- **input** (string) - Required - The user input or question
- **tools** (array) - Optional - Array of tool objects to enable
  - **type** (string) - Tool type (e.g., 'web_search')

### Request Example
```javascript
const answer = await client.responses.create({
  model: 'gpt-5',
  input: 'Who is the current president of France?',
  tools: [{ type: 'web_search' }]
});

console.log(answer.output_text);
```

### Response
#### Success Response (200)
- **id** (string) - Unique response identifier
- **output_text** (string) - The generated response text
- **model** (string) - Model used for generation
- **usage** (object) - Token usage information
  - **input_tokens** (integer) - Tokens used in input
  - **output_tokens** (integer) - Tokens used in output

### Response Example
```json
{
  "id": "resp-123",
  "output_text": "The current president of France is Emmanuel Macron. He has been in office since May 2017 and was re-elected in 2022.",
  "model": "gpt-5",
  "usage": {
    "input_tokens": 15,
    "output_tokens": 35
  }
}
```
```

--------------------------------

### POST /v1/responses - Web Search Tool

Source: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/tools-web-search_api-mode=responses

Create a response with web search enabled using the Responses API. The model can choose to search the web based on the input prompt. This endpoint supports three types of web search: non-reasoning for quick lookups, agentic search with reasoning models for complex workflows, and deep research for extended investigations.

```APIDOC
## POST /v1/responses

### Description
Create a response with web search capability enabled. The model will have access to web search tools and can choose to search the web based on the input prompt to provide up-to-date information with sourced citations.

### Method
POST

### Endpoint
https://api.openai.com/v1/responses

### Parameters
#### Request Body
- **model** (string) - Required - The model to use (e.g., "gpt-5", "o3-deep-research", "o4-mini-deep-research")
- **tools** (array) - Required - Array of tools to enable. Include {"type": "web_search"} to enable web search
- **input** (string) - Required - The user's query or prompt for the model

### Request Example
```json
{
  "model": "gpt-5",
  "tools": [
    {"type": "web_search"}
  ],
  "input": "What was a positive news story from today?"
}
```

### Response
#### Success Response (200)
- **output_text** (string) - The model's response based on web search results with sourced citations
- **model** (string) - The model used for the response
- **tools_used** (array) - Array of tools that were utilized by the model

#### Response Example
```json
{
  "output_text": "[Model response with web search results and citations]",
  "model": "gpt-5",
  "tools_used": ["web_search"]
}
```

### Web Search Types
1. **Non-reasoning web search** - Fast method for quick lookups. Model sends query to web search tool and returns top results without internal planning.
2. **Agentic search with reasoning models** - Model actively manages search process, analyzes results, and decides whether to continue searching. Better for complex workflows.
3. **Deep research** - Specialized agent-driven method for in-depth investigations. Can tap into hundreds of sources and run for several minutes. Best used with background mode.

### Code Examples

#### JavaScript
```javascript
import OpenAI from "openai";
const client = new OpenAI();

const response = await client.responses.create({
    model: "gpt-5",
    tools: [
        { type: "web_search" },
    ],
    input: "What was a positive news story from today?",
});

console.log(response.output_text);
```

#### Python
```python
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

response = client.responses.create(
    model="gpt-5",
    tools=[{"type": "web_search"}],
    input="What was a positive news story from today?"
)

print(response.output_text)
```

#### cURL
```bash
curl "https://api.openai.com/v1/responses" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY" \
    -d '{
        "model": "gpt-5",
        "tools": [{"type": "web_search"}],
        "input": "what was a positive news story from today?"
    }'
```

#### C#
```csharp
using OpenAI.Responses;

string key = Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable("OPENAI_API_KEY")!;
OpenAIResponseClient client = new(model: "gpt-5", apiKey: key);

ResponseCreationOptions options = new();
options.Tools.Add(ResponseTool.CreateWebSearchTool());

OpenAIResponse response = (OpenAIResponse)client.CreateResponse([
    ResponseItem.CreateUserMessageItem([
        ResponseContentPart.CreateInputTextPart("What was a positive news story from today?"),
    ]),
], options);

Console.WriteLine(response.GetOutputText());
```
```

--------------------------------

### POST /v1/responses (Web Search)

Source: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/tools

This endpoint allows you to create a model response that includes web search results. By specifying "web_search" as a tool, the model can access up-to-date information from the internet to inform its output.

```APIDOC
## POST /v1/responses

### Description
This endpoint allows you to create a model response that includes web search results. By specifying "web_search" as a tool, the model can access up-to-date information from the internet to inform its output.

### Method
POST

### Endpoint
/v1/responses

### Parameters
#### Path Parameters
(None)

#### Query Parameters
(None)

#### Request Body
- **model** (string) - Required - The ID of the model to use.
- **tools** (array of objects) - Required - A list of tools the model may use. For web search, include an object with `type: "web_search"`.
- **input** (string or array of objects) - Required - The user's input message.

### Request Example

  "tools": [{"type": "web_search"}],
  "input": "what was a positive news story from today?"
}

### Response

#### Response Example

  "output_text": "A positive news story from today is that scientists have made a breakthrough in..."
}
```

--------------------------------

### POST /v1/responses - Web Search Tool

Source: https://platform.openai.com/docs/quickstart_api-mode=responses

This endpoint allows you to use the OpenAI model with a built-in web search tool. The model can then access external information from the web to formulate its response.

```APIDOC

### Description
This endpoint allows you to use the OpenAI model with a built-in web search tool. The model can then access external information from the web to formulate its response.

content.text:
Now I have the full picture. Key finding: OpenAI's web search in Chat Completions API uses `web_search_options: {}` as a top-level parameter (not a tool in the `tools` array). This is similar to Gemini's `GoogleSearch` grounding — it's a model-level feature, not a function call. This actually simplifies the design significantly.

Now I have enough context to propose approaches.

content.content: Updated task #3 status
Updated task #4 status

content.text:
Here are my proposed approaches — all three unify the agent architecture so both providers use `agenttool`, but they differ in how OpenAI web search is wired in.

---

## Approach A: Mirror Gemini's pattern exactly — search as a sub-agent (Recommended)

Both providers get the same 3-agent structure: root → data sub-agent + search sub-agent.

- **Gemini search sub-agent** uses `geminitool.GoogleSearch{}` (as today)
- **OpenAI search sub-agent** uses `web_search_options: {}` in the chat completion request (model-level parameter, same idea as Gemini grounding)

The OpenAI wrapper already translates `genai.Tool` entries. We'd add support for recognizing a `GoogleSearch`-equivalent marker in the request config and emitting `web_search_options: {}` in the HTTP body instead.

**Trade-offs:**
- Maximum code reuse — `BuildAgent()` becomes one path with a provider-specific search tool swap
- `web_search_options` only works with specific OpenAI models (`gpt-4o-search-preview`, `gpt-5-search-api`) — forces model selection for search sub-agent
- The search sub-agent would need its own model instance (search-capable model), different from the main model

## Approach B: OpenAI search as a function tool (no sub-agent)

Same 3-agent structure, but the OpenAI search sub-agent uses a custom `functiontool` that calls an external search API (Tavily, Brave, SerpAPI).

**Trade-offs:**
- No model restrictions — any OpenAI model works
- Adds an external API dependency + API key
- OpenAI doesn't control the search; the tool returns raw results for the LLM to interpret

## Approach C: Search via model config, not sub-agent

Instead of a search sub-agent, both providers enable search as a model-level config. Gemini gets `geminitool.GoogleSearch{}` as a tool on the root agent. OpenAI gets `web_search_options` on the root agent's requests.

Use `agenttool` only for the data sub-agent (both providers). No search sub-agent at all.

**Trade-offs:**
- Simplest architecture (root + 1 data sub-agent)
- Gemini can't mix grounding tools with function tools on the same agent — meaning the root agent can't have both `agenttool` (data sub-agent) and `GoogleSearch`. **This doesn't work for Gemini.**

---

**My recommendation: Approach A.** It mirrors the existing Gemini pattern exactly and uses OpenAI's native capability. The search model constraint is manageable — you already configure models per provider. The search sub-agent would just use a search-capable model while the root and data agents use the configured `LLM_MODEL`.

Does this approach work for you, or would you prefer a different direction?

Good. Let me now present the design section by section.

## Section 1: Unified `BuildAgent()` Architecture

The core change: `BuildAgent()` becomes a single code path for both providers. The only difference is which search tool the search sub-agent gets.

```
Root Agent (release_analyst)
├── Data Sub-Agent (data_agent) — agenttool
│   └── functiontools: get_releases, get_release_detail, list_context_sources
└── Search Sub-Agent (search_agent) — agenttool
    └── Gemini: geminitool.GoogleSearch{}
        OpenAI: openaitool.WebSearch{}  ← new
```

`BuildAgent()` stops branching on provider. Instead, it builds a `searchTool` based on provider and passes it to the same sub-agent structure.

Does this section look right?

## Section 2: OpenAI Web Search Implementation

OpenAI's Chat Completions web search uses `web_search_options: {}` as a top-level request parameter — not a tool in the `tools` array. This is analogous to how Gemini's `GoogleSearch` is a server-side grounding feature rather than a function call.

**New type: `openaitool.WebSearch{}`**

A struct in `internal/agent/openai/` that implements ADK's `tool.Tool` interface, mirroring how `geminitool.GoogleSearch{}` works. It:

1. Returns a `nil` FunctionDeclaration (no function schema — it's not a callable tool)
2. In `ProcessRequest()`, sets a marker on the request that the OpenAI wrapper can detect
3. The OpenAI wrapper's `convertTools()` / `doRequest()` recognizes this marker and emits `"web_search_options": {}` in the HTTP body

**Model constraint:** `web_search_options` only works with search-capable models (`gpt-4o-search-preview`, `gpt-4o-mini-search-preview`, `gpt-5-search-api`). The search sub-agent needs its own model instance with a search-capable model name. This means `LLMConfig` gets a new optional field:

```go
type LLMConfig struct {
    // ... existing fields ...
    OpenAISearchModel string // e.g. "gpt-5-search-api", defaults to "gpt-5-search-api"
}
```

**Schema casing fix:** `schemaToMap()` in the OpenAI wrapper lowercases the `Type` field so `agenttool`'s `"OBJECT"` / `"STRING"` schemas become `"object"` / `"string"`.

Does this section look right?

## Section 3: OpenAI Wrapper Changes

Three changes to `internal/agent/openai/openai.go`:

**1. Schema type lowercasing in `schemaToMap()`**

```go
if s.Type != "" {
    m["type"] = strings.ToLower(string(s.Type))
}
```

**2. Web search options support in `chatRequest`**

Add a `WebSearchOptions` field to the request struct:

```go
type chatRequest struct {
    // ... existing fields ...
    WebSearchOptions *webSearchOptions `json:"web_search_options,omitempty"`
}

type webSearchOptions struct{} // empty object — OpenAI just needs `{}`
```

**3. Detect web search marker in `doRequest()`**

Before building the HTTP request, check if any `genai.Tool` in the request config has `GoogleSearch` set (or our custom marker). If found, set `chatReq.WebSearchOptions = &webSearchOptions{}` and skip that tool entry in `convertTools()`.

This keeps the OpenAI wrapper self-contained — no changes needed to ADK internals.

Does this section look right?

## Section 5: Environment Variables & Config

One new env var:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `OPENAI_SEARCH_MODEL` | `gpt-5-search-api` | Search-capable model for OpenAI search sub-agent |

`LLMConfig` gets one new field:

```go
type LLMConfig struct {
    // ... existing ...
    OpenAISearchModel string
}
```

Wired in `cmd/server/main.go` and `cmd/agent/main.go`:

```go
openaiSearchModel := envOr("OPENAI_SEARCH_MODEL", "gpt-5-search-api")
```

The search sub-agent creates its own `model.LLM` instance via `NewLLMModel()` with the search model name. The root and data agents continue using `LLM_MODEL`.

For Gemini, this field is ignored — all agents use the same model (Gemini handles search grounding server-side on any model).

Does this section look right?

## Section 6: Test Strategy

**Unit tests:**

1. **`schemaToMap()` lowercasing** — verify `"OBJECT"` → `"object"`, `"STRING"` → `"string"` in the output map
2. **`convertTools()` with GoogleSearch marker** — verify it emits `web_search_options` and skips the marker tool from the `tools` array
3. **`WebSearch.ProcessRequest()`** — verify it adds the correct `genai.Tool{GoogleSearch: ...}` entry to the request config
4. **`WebSearch.Declaration()`** — verify it returns `nil`

**Integration test (manual):**

- Run the agent dev entrypoint (`cmd/agent`) with `LLM_PROVIDER=openai` and verify:
  - Data sub-agent tool calls work (agenttool round-trip)
  - Search sub-agent triggers web search (visible in response annotations)
  - Final semantic report is produced correctly

No new external dependencies. No database schema changes.

Does this section look right?
Good. The full design is approved. Let me write the design doc and commit it.

input.content:
# Unified Agent Architecture Design

## Goal

Unify the agent flow so both Gemini and OpenAI providers use the same sub-agent architecture via `agenttool`. Currently Gemini uses a 3-agent hierarchy (root + data sub-agent + search sub-agent) while OpenAI uses a flat architecture with all tools on the root agent. After this change, both providers share the same structure — the only difference is which search tool the search sub-agent receives.

## Architecture

```
Root Agent (release_analyst)
├── Data Sub-Agent (data_agent) — via agenttool
│   └── functiontools: get_releases, get_release_detail, list_context_sources
└── Search Sub-Agent (search_agent) — via agenttool
    └── Gemini: geminitool.GoogleSearch{}
        OpenAI: openaitool.WebSearch{}  ← new
```

`BuildAgent()` becomes a single code path. It builds a provider-specific `searchTool` and passes it to the same sub-agent structure.

## OpenAI Web Search

OpenAI's Chat Completions web search uses `web_search_options: {}` as a top-level request parameter (not a tool in the `tools` array). This mirrors how Gemini's `GoogleSearch` is a server-side grounding feature.

### New type: `WebSearch` in `internal/agent/openai/websearch.go`

Implements `tool.Tool`. Key behaviors:

- `Declaration()` returns `nil` (not a callable function)
- `ProcessRequest()` adds `genai.Tool{GoogleSearch: &genai.GoogleSearch{}}` to the request config — reusing the same marker that `geminitool.GoogleSearch{}` uses
- `Run()` returns an error (never called directly; search happens inside the LLM call)

The OpenAI wrapper detects `GoogleSearch != nil` on any `genai.Tool` entry and translates it to `web_search_options: {}` in the HTTP body.

### Model constraint

`web_search_options` only works with search-capable models (`gpt-4o-search-preview`, `gpt-4o-mini-search-preview`, `gpt-5-search-api`). The search sub-agent gets its own model instance with a search-capable model name.

## OpenAI Wrapper Changes (`internal/agent/openai/openai.go`)

### 1. Schema type lowercasing in `schemaToMap()`

`agenttool` generates schemas with uppercase types (`"OBJECT"`, `"STRING"`). OpenAI expects lowercase. Fix: `strings.ToLower(string(s.Type))`.

### 2. Web search options in `chatRequest`

```go
type chatRequest struct {
    // ... existing fields ...
    WebSearchOptions *webSearchOptions `json:"web_search_options,omitempty"`
}

type webSearchOptions struct{}
```

### 3. Detect GoogleSearch marker in `doRequest()`

Before building the HTTP request, check if any `genai.Tool` has `GoogleSearch` set. If found, set `chatReq.WebSearchOptions = &webSearchOptions{}` and skip that tool entry in `convertTools()`.

## Config Changes

New env var:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `OPENAI_SEARCH_MODEL` | `gpt-5-search-api` | Search-capable model for OpenAI search sub-agent |

New field in `LLMConfig`:

```go
OpenAISearchModel string
```

Wired in `cmd/server/main.go` and `cmd/agent/main.go`. For Gemini, this field is ignored.

## Test Strategy

### Unit tests

1. `schemaToMap()` — verify `"OBJECT"` → `"object"`, `"STRING"` → `"string"`
2. `convertTools()` with GoogleSearch marker — verify `web_search_options` emitted, marker skipped
3. `WebSearch.ProcessRequest()` — verify correct `genai.Tool` entry added
4. `WebSearch.Declaration()` — verify returns `nil`

### Manual integration test

Run `cmd/agent` with `LLM_PROVIDER=openai` and verify:
- Data sub-agent tool calls work (agenttool round-trip)
- Search sub-agent triggers web search
- Final semantic report produced correctly

## Non-goals

- No new external dependencies
- No database schema changes
- No changes to Gemini flow behavior

Base directory for this skill: /Users/pc/.claude/plugins/cache/superpowers-marketplace/superpowers/4.3.1/skills/writing-plans

# Writing Plans

## Overview

Write comprehensive implementation plans assuming the engineer has zero context for our codebase and questionable taste. Document everything they need to know: which files to touch for each task, code, testing, docs they might need to check, how to test it. Give them the whole plan as bite-sized tasks. DRY. YAGNI. TDD. Frequent commits.

Assume they are a skilled developer, but know almost nothing about our toolset or problem domain. Assume they don't know good test design very well.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the writing-plans skill to create the implementation plan."

**Context:** This should be run in a dedicated worktree (created by brainstorming skill).

**Save plans to:** `docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<feature-name>.md`

## Bite-Sized Task Granularity

**Each step is one action (2-5 minutes):**
- "Write the failing test" - step
- "Run it to make sure it fails" - step
- "Implement the minimal code to make the test pass" - step
- "Run the tests and make sure they pass" - step
- "Commit" - step

## Plan Document Header

**Every plan MUST start with this header:**

```markdown
# [Feature Name] Implementation Plan

> **For Claude:** REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task.

**Goal:** [One sentence describing what this builds]

**Architecture:** [2-3 sentences about approach]

**Tech Stack:** [Key technologies/libraries]

---
```

## Task Structure

````markdown
### Task N: [Component Name]

**Files:**
- Create: `exact/path/to/file.py`
- Modify: `exact/path/to/existing.py:123-145`
- Test: `tests/exact/path/to/test.py`

**Step 1: Write the failing test**

```python
def test_specific_behavior():
    result = function(input)
    assert result == expected
```

**Step 2: Run test to verify it fails**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: FAIL with "function not defined"

**Step 3: Write minimal implementation**

```python
def function(input):
    return expected
```

**Step 4: Run test to verify it passes**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: PASS

**Step 5: Commit**

```bash
git add tests/path/test.py src/path/file.py
git commit -m "feat: add specific feature"
```
````

## Remember
- Exact file paths always
- Complete code in plan (not "add validation")
- Exact commands with expected output
- Reference relevant skills with @ syntax
- DRY, YAGNI, TDD, frequent commits

## Execution Handoff

After saving the plan, offer execution choice:

**"Plan complete and saved to `docs/plans/<filename>.md`. Two execution options:**

**1. Subagent-Driven (this session)** - I dispatch fresh subagent per task, review between tasks, fast iteration

**2. Parallel Session (separate)** - Open new session with executing-plans, batch execution with checkpoints

**Which approach?"**

**If Subagent-Driven chosen:**
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development
- Stay in this session
- Fresh subagent per task + code review

**If Parallel Session chosen:**
- Guide them to open new session in worktree
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** New session uses superpowers:executing-plans

file.content:
# Unified Agent Architecture Design

## Goal

Unify the agent flow so both Gemini and OpenAI providers use the same sub-agent architecture via `agenttool`. Currently Gemini uses a 3-agent hierarchy (root + data sub-agent + search sub-agent) while OpenAI uses a flat architecture with all tools on the root agent. After this change, both providers share the same structure — the only difference is which search tool the search sub-agent receives.

## Architecture

```
Root Agent (release_analyst)
├── Data Sub-Agent (data_agent) — via agenttool
│   └── functiontools: get_releases, get_release_detail, list_context_sources
└── Search Sub-Agent (search_agent) — via agenttool
    └── Gemini: geminitool.GoogleSearch{}
        OpenAI: openaitool.WebSearch{}  ← new
```

`BuildAgent()` becomes a single code path. It builds a provider-specific `searchTool` and passes it to the same sub-agent structure.

## OpenAI Web Search

OpenAI's Chat Completions web search uses `web_search_options: {}` as a top-level request parameter (not a tool in the `tools` array). This mirrors how Gemini's `GoogleSearch` is a server-side grounding feature.

### New type: `WebSearch` in `internal/agent/openai/websearch.go`

Implements `tool.Tool`. Key behaviors:

- `Declaration()` returns `nil` (not a callable function)
- `ProcessRequest()` adds `genai.Tool{GoogleSearch: &genai.GoogleSearch{}}` to the request config — reusing the same marker that `geminitool.GoogleSearch{}` uses
- `Run()` returns an error (never called directly; search happens inside the LLM call)

The OpenAI wrapper detects `GoogleSearch != nil` on any `genai.Tool` entry and translates it to `web_search_options: {}` in the HTTP body.

### Model constraint

`web_search_options` only works with search-capable models (`gpt-4o-search-preview`, `gpt-4o-mini-search-preview`, `gpt-5-search-api`). The search sub-agent gets its own model instance with a search-capable model name.

## OpenAI Wrapper Changes (`internal/agent/openai/openai.go`)

### 1. Schema type lowercasing in `schemaToMap()`

`agenttool` generates schemas with uppercase types (`"OBJECT"`, `"STRING"`). OpenAI expects lowercase. Fix: `strings.ToLower(string(s.Type))`.

### 2. Web search options in `chatRequest`

```go
type chatRequest struct {
    // ... existing fields ...
    WebSearchOptions *webSearchOptions `json:"web_search_options,omitempty"`
}

type webSearchOptions struct{}
```

### 3. Detect GoogleSearch marker in `doRequest()`

Before building the HTTP request, check if any `genai.Tool` has `GoogleSearch` set. If found, set `chatReq.WebSearchOptions = &webSearchOptions{}` and skip that tool entry in `convertTools()`.

## Config Changes

New env var:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `OPENAI_SEARCH_MODEL` | `gpt-5-search-api` | Search-capable model for OpenAI search sub-agent |

New field in `LLMConfig`:

```go
OpenAISearchModel string
```

Wired in `cmd/server/main.go` and `cmd/agent/main.go`. For Gemini, this field is ignored.

## Test Strategy

### Unit tests

1. `schemaToMap()` — verify `"OBJECT"` → `"object"`, `"STRING"` → `"string"`
2. `convertTools()` with GoogleSearch marker — verify `web_search_options` emitted, marker skipped
3. `WebSearch.ProcessRequest()` — verify correct `genai.Tool` entry added
4. `WebSearch.Declaration()` — verify returns `nil`

### Manual integration test

Run `cmd/agent` with `LLM_PROVIDER=openai` and verify:
- Data sub-agent tool calls work (agenttool round-trip)
- Search sub-agent triggers web search
- Final semantic report produced correctly

## Non-goals

- No new external dependencies
- No database schema changes
- No changes to Gemini flow behavior

1→package agent
     2→
     3→import (
     4→	"context"
     5→	"fmt"
     6→
     7→	"google.golang.org/adk/model"
     8→	"google.golang.org/adk/model/gemini"
     9→	"google.golang.org/genai"
    10→
    11→	oaimodel "github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/agent/openai"
    12→)
    13→
    14→// LLMConfig holds the configuration for creating an LLM model.
    15→type LLMConfig struct {
    16→	Provider string // "gemini" or "openai"
    17→	Model    string // e.g. "gemini-2.5-flash", "gpt-5.2"
    18→
    19→	// Gemini
    20→	GoogleAPIKey string
    21→
    22→	// OpenAI
    23→	OpenAIAPIKey  string
    24→	OpenAIBaseURL string // defaults to https://api.openai.com/v1
    25→}
    26→
    27→// NewLLMModel creates a model.LLM based on the configured provider.
    28→func NewLLMModel(ctx context.Context, cfg LLMConfig) (model.LLM, error) {
    29→	switch cfg.Provider {
    30→	case "gemini", "":
    31→		if cfg.GoogleAPIKey == "" {
    32→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("GOOGLE_API_KEY is required for gemini provider")
    33→		}
    34→		return gemini.NewModel(ctx, cfg.Model, &genai.ClientConfig{
    35→			APIKey: cfg.GoogleAPIKey,
    36→		})
    37→
    38→	case "openai":
    39→		return oaimodel.NewModel(ctx, cfg.Model, oaimodel.Config{
    40→			APIKey:  cfg.OpenAIAPIKey,
    41→			BaseURL: cfg.OpenAIBaseURL,
    42→		})
    43→
    44→	default:
    45→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("unknown LLM provider: %q (supported: gemini, openai)", cfg.Provider)
    46→	}
    47→}
    48→

1→package agent
     2→
     3→import (
     4→	"context"
     5→	"encoding/json"
     6→	"fmt"
     7→	"log/slog"
     8→	"net/http"
     9→	"strings"
    10→	"time"
    11→
    12→	"google.golang.org/genai"
    13→
    14→	"google.golang.org/adk/agent"
    15→	"google.golang.org/adk/agent/llmagent"
    16→	"google.golang.org/adk/plugin"
    17→	"google.golang.org/adk/plugin/loggingplugin"
    18→	"google.golang.org/adk/runner"
    19→	"google.golang.org/adk/session"
    20→	"google.golang.org/adk/tool"
    21→	"google.golang.org/adk/tool/agenttool"
    22→	"google.golang.org/adk/tool/geminitool"
    23→
    24→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/models"
    25→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/routing"
    26→)
    27→
    28→// OrchestratorStore defines all data access methods required by the agent
    29→// orchestrator to load project configuration, run the agent, and persist
    30→// the resulting semantic release.
    31→type OrchestratorStore interface {
    32→	AgentDataStore
    33→	GetProject(ctx context.Context, id string) (*models.Project, error)
    34→	GetAgentRun(ctx context.Context, id string) (*models.AgentRun, error)
    35→	UpdateAgentRunStatus(ctx context.Context, id, status string) error
    36→	CreateSemanticRelease(ctx context.Context, sr *models.SemanticRelease, releaseIDs []string) error
    37→	UpdateAgentRunResult(ctx context.Context, id string, semanticReleaseID string) error
    38→	ListProjectSubscriptions(ctx context.Context, projectID string) ([]models.Subscription, error)
    39→	GetChannel(ctx context.Context, id string) (*models.NotificationChannel, error)
    40→	ListSourcesByProject(ctx context.Context, projectID string, page, perPage int) ([]models.Source, int, error)
    41→	HasReleaseForVersion(ctx context.Context, sourceID, version string) (bool, error)
    42→}
    43→
    44→const DefaultInstruction = `You are a release intelligence agent analyzing version {{VERSION}} of a software project.
    45→
    46→Focus ONLY on version {{VERSION}}. Cross-check this version across all available sources.
    47→
    48→Use the available tools to:
    49→1. Fetch releases and find the one matching {{VERSION}} from each source.
    50→2. Inspect release details (changelogs, commit data, raw payloads) for {{VERSION}} only.
    51→3. Check binary/image availability directly from the source data.
    52→4. Review the project's context sources (runbooks, documentation) for relevant background.
    53→5. Use web search ONLY when you need additional context not available from sources
    54→   (e.g., community sentiment, security advisories, network adoption stats, known issues).
    55→
    56→CRITICAL: Your final response MUST be a single JSON object and nothing else.
    57→Do not include any explanation, commentary, or markdown formatting — just the raw JSON.
    58→
    59→For download_links: prefer direct binary/artifact URLs for specific platforms (e.g., linux-amd64, darwin-arm64, windows-amd64 .tar.gz/.zip files) over generic release pages. Include the general release page URL as well, but prioritize direct download links when available.
    60→
    61→The JSON object must have exactly these fields:
    62→{
    63→  "subject": "Ready to Deploy: <Project> <Version> (<Risk Summary>)",
    64→  "risk_level": "CRITICAL|HIGH|MEDIUM|LOW",
    65→  "risk_reason": "Why this risk level (e.g., 'Hard Fork detected in Discord #announcements')",
    66→  "status_checks": ["Docker Image Verified", "Binaries Available"],
    67→  "changelog_summary": "One-line summary of key changes (e.g., 'Fixes sync bug in block 14,000,000')",
    68→  "availability": "GA|RC|Beta",
    69→  "adoption": "Percentage or recommendation (e.g., '12% of network updated (Wait recommended if not urgent)')",
    70→  "urgency": "Critical|High|Medium|Low",
    71→  "recommendation": "Actionable 1-2 sentence recommendation for the SRE team",
    72→  "download_commands": ["docker pull ethereum/client-go:v1.10.15"],
    73→  "download_links": ["https://gethstore.blob.core.windows.net/builds/geth-linux-amd64-1.10.15-8be800ff.tar.gz", "https://gethstore.blob.core.windows.net/builds/geth-darwin-arm64-1.10.15-8be800ff.tar.gz", "https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/releases/tag/v1.10.15"]
    74→}`
    75→
    76→// BuildAgent creates the ADK-Go LLM agent for a given project. This is used
    77→// by both the production orchestrator and the dev entrypoint to ensure the
    78→// same agent configuration is used everywhere.
    79→func BuildAgent(ctx context.Context, store AgentDataStore, project *models.Project, llmConfig LLMConfig, version string) (agent.Agent, error) {
    80→	instruction := DefaultInstruction
    81→	if project.AgentPrompt != "" {
    82→		instruction = project.AgentPrompt + "\n\n" + instruction
    83→	}
    84→	// Substitute version placeholder
    85→	instruction = strings.ReplaceAll(instruction, "{{VERSION}}", version)
    86→
    87→	llmModel, err := NewLLMModel(ctx, llmConfig)
    88→	if err != nil {
    89→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("create LLM model: %w", err)
    90→	}
    91→
    92→	// Create project-scoped function tools.
    93→	functionTools, err := NewTools(store, project.ID)
    94→	if err != nil {
    95→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("create agent tools: %w", err)
    96→	}
    97→
    98→	// For Gemini: use sub-agent pattern (required because Gemini can't mix
    99→	// grounding tools like GoogleSearch with function tools on the same agent).
   100→	// For OpenAI: flat architecture — give function tools directly to root agent
   101→	// (OpenAI doesn't support the agenttool schema).
   102→	if llmConfig.Provider == "openai" {
   103→		return llmagent.New(llmagent.Config{
   104→			Name:        "release_analyst",
   105→			Description: "Analyzes upstream releases and produces semantic release reports.",
   106→			Model:       llmModel,
   107→			Instruction: instruction,
   108→			Tools:       functionTools,
   109→		})
   110→	}
   111→
   112→	// Gemini path: sub-agent architecture.
   113→
   114→	// Data sub-agent: handles DB queries for releases and context sources.
   115→	dataAgent, err := llmagent.New(llmagent.Config{
   116→		Name:        "data_agent",
   117→		Description: "Query project releases and context sources from the database. Use this to fetch release lists, release details, and context sources like runbooks and documentation.",
   118→		Model:       llmModel,
   119→		Tools:       functionTools,
   120→	})
   121→	if err != nil {
   122→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("create data sub-agent: %w", err)
   123→	}
   124→
   125→	// Search sub-agent: Google Search grounding.
   126→	searchAgent, err := llmagent.New(llmagent.Config{
   127→		Name:        "search_agent",
   128→		Description: "Search the web for additional context about a release. Use this ONLY when you need information not available from the project's sources, such as community sentiment, security advisories, network adoption statistics, or known issues.",
   129→		Model:       llmModel,
   130→		Tools:       []tool.Tool{geminitool.GoogleSearch{}},
   131→	})
   132→	if err != nil {
   133→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("create search sub-agent: %w", err)
   134→	}
   135→
   136→	// Root agent orchestrates data lookup and web search via sub-agents.
   137→	return llmagent.New(llmagent.Config{
   138→		Name:        "release_analyst",
   139→		Description: "Analyzes upstream releases and produces semantic release reports.",
   140→		Model:       llmModel,
   141→		Instruction: instruction,
   142→		Tools: []tool.Tool{
   143→			agenttool.New(dataAgent, nil),
   144→			agenttool.New(searchAgent, nil),
   145→		},
   146→	})
   147→}
   148→
   149→// checkAllSourcesReady returns true if every source in the project has a
   150→// release matching the target version.
   151→func (o *Orchestrator) checkAllSourcesReady(ctx context.Context, projectID, version string) (bool, error) {
   152→	// Use a high limit to fetch all sources; projects rarely exceed a handful.
   153→	sources, _, err := o.store.ListSourcesByProject(ctx, projectID, 1, 1000)
   154→	if err != nil {
   155→		return false, fmt.Errorf("list sources: %w", err)
   156→	}
   157→	if len(sources) == 0 {
   158→		return true, nil
   159→	}
   160→
   161→	for _, src := range sources {
   162→		has, err := o.store.HasReleaseForVersion(ctx, src.ID, version)
   163→		if err != nil {
   164→			return false, fmt.Errorf("check source %s: %w", src.ID, err)
   165→		}
   166→		if !has {
   167→			slog.Info("agent: source not ready for version",
   168→				"project_id", projectID,
   169→				"source_id", src.ID,
   170→				"version", version,
   171→			)
   172→			return false, nil
   173→		}
   174→	}
   175→	return true, nil
   176→}
   177→
   178→// Orchestrator manages the lifecycle of an agent run: loading project config,
   179→// creating an ADK-Go agent with project-specific tools and instructions,
   180→// running the agent, and persisting the result as a semantic release.
   181→type Orchestrator struct {
   182→	store     OrchestratorStore
   183→	llmConfig LLMConfig
   184→}
   185→
   186→// NewOrchestrator creates a new Orchestrator. It validates that the provided
   187→// LLMConfig has the required API key for the configured provider. If the key
   188→// is missing, it returns an error so the caller can degrade gracefully.
   189→func NewOrchestrator(store OrchestratorStore, cfg LLMConfig) (*Orchestrator, error) {
   190→	switch cfg.Provider {
   191→	case "openai":
   192→		if cfg.OpenAIAPIKey == "" {
   193→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("OPENAI_API_KEY is not set; agent orchestrator requires an LLM API key")
   194→		}
   195→	default: // gemini
   196→		if cfg.GoogleAPIKey == "" {
   197→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("GOOGLE_API_KEY is not set; agent orchestrator requires an LLM API key")
   198→		}
   199→	}
   200→	return &Orchestrator{
   201→		store:     store,
   202→		llmConfig: cfg,
   203→	}, nil
   204→}
   205→
   206→// RunAgent executes the full agent lifecycle for a given agent run:
   207→//  1. Marks the run as "running".
   208→//  2. Loads the project and builds the agent prompt.
   209→//  3. Creates an ADK-Go LLM agent with project-scoped tools.
   210→//  4. Runs the agent and captures its final text response.
   211→//  5. Parses the response into a SemanticReport.
   212→//  6. Creates a SemanticRelease and links it to the agent run.
   213→//  7. Marks the run as "completed" (or "failed" on error).
   214→func (o *Orchestrator) RunAgent(ctx context.Context, run *models.AgentRun) error {
   215→	// Mark as running.
   216→	if err := o.store.UpdateAgentRunStatus(ctx, run.ID, "running"); err != nil {
   217→		return fmt.Errorf("update agent run status to running: %w", err)
   218→	}
   219→
   220→	// Execute the agent; capture any error to mark the run as failed.
   221→	result, err := o.executeAgent(ctx, run)
   222→	if err != nil {
   223→		slog.Error("agent run failed", "run_id", run.ID, "err", err)
   224→		// Best-effort: mark the run as failed.
   225→		if statusErr := o.store.UpdateAgentRunStatus(ctx, run.ID, "failed"); statusErr != nil {
   226→			slog.Error("agent: failed to mark run as failed",
   227→				"run_id", run.ID, "status_err", statusErr)
   228→		}
   229→		return err
   230→	}
   231→
   232→	// Link the semantic release to the agent run and mark completed.
   233→	if err := o.store.UpdateAgentRunResult(ctx, run.ID, result.semanticReleaseID); err != nil {
   234→		return fmt.Errorf("update agent run result: %w", err)
   235→	}
   236→	if err := o.store.UpdateAgentRunStatus(ctx, run.ID, "completed"); err != nil {
   237→		return fmt.Errorf("update agent run status to completed: %w", err)
   238→	}
   239→
   240→	// Send project-level notifications (best-effort; errors are logged, not returned).
   241→	o.sendProjectNotifications(ctx, run, result)
   242→
   243→	return nil
   244→}
   245→
   246→// agentResult holds the output of a successful agent execution, used to
   247→// construct notifications after the semantic release is persisted.
   248→type agentResult struct {
   249→	semanticReleaseID string
   250→	version           string
   251→	reportText        string
   252→	projectName       string
   253→}
   254→
   255→// executeAgent performs the actual LLM agent interaction and returns the
   256→// agentResult containing the semantic release ID and metadata for notifications.
   257→func (o *Orchestrator) executeAgent(ctx context.Context, run *models.AgentRun) (*agentResult, error) {
   258→	// Load project.
   259→	slog.Info("agent: loading project", "run_id", run.ID, "project_id", run.ProjectID)
   260→	project, err := o.store.GetProject(ctx, run.ProjectID)
   261→	if err != nil {
   262→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("get project: %w", err)
   263→	}
   264→	slog.Info("agent: project loaded", "run_id", run.ID, "project", project.Name)
   265→
   266→	// Extract target version from the agent run.
   267→	version := run.Version
   268→	if version == "" {
   269→		// Fallback: parse from trigger "auto:version:v1.10.15"
   270→		if strings.HasPrefix(run.Trigger, "auto:version:") {
   271→			version = strings.TrimPrefix(run.Trigger, "auto:version:")
   272→		}
   273→	}
   274→
   275→	// Build the agent using the shared constructor.
   276→	slog.Info("agent: building agent", "run_id", run.ID,
   277→		"provider", o.llmConfig.Provider, "model", o.llmConfig.Model,
   278→		"version", version)
   279→	agentInstance, err := BuildAgent(ctx, o.store, project, o.llmConfig, version)
   280→	if err != nil {
   281→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("build agent: %w", err)
   282→	}
   283→
   284→	// Create in-memory session service and a new session.
   285→	sessionService := session.InMemoryService()
   286→	createResp, err := sessionService.Create(ctx, &session.CreateRequest{
   287→		AppName: "changelogue",
   288→		UserID:  "system",
   289→	})
   290→	if err != nil {
   291→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("create session: %w", err)
   292→	}
   293→	sess := createResp.Session
   294→
   295→	// Create the runner with logging plugin for agent trace visibility.
   296→	r, err := runner.New(runner.Config{
   297→		AppName:        "changelogue",
   298→		Agent:          agentInstance,
   299→		SessionService: sessionService,
   300→		PluginConfig: runner.PluginConfig{
   301→			Plugins:      []*plugin.Plugin{loggingplugin.MustNew("agent_trace")},
   302→			CloseTimeout: 5 * time.Second,
   303→		},
   304→	})
   305→	if err != nil {
   306→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("create runner: %w", err)
   307→	}
   308→
   309→	// Run the agent with a prompt requesting analysis.
   310→	slog.Info("agent: starting LLM run", "run_id", run.ID, "project", project.Name, "version", version)
   311→	var userPrompt string
   312→	if version != "" {
   313→		userPrompt = fmt.Sprintf("Analyze version %s for this project. Cross-check all sources and produce a semantic release report.", version)
   314→	} else {
   315→		userPrompt = "Analyze the recent releases for this project and produce a semantic release report."
   316→	}
   317→	userMsg := genai.NewContentFromText(userPrompt, "user")
   318→
   319→	var finalText string
   320→	eventCount := 0
   321→	for event, err := range r.Run(ctx, "system", sess.ID(), userMsg, agent.RunConfig{}) {
   322→		if err != nil {
   323→			slog.Error("agent: run event error",
   324→				"run_id", run.ID,
   325→				"event_count", eventCount,
   326→				"err", err,
   327→			)
   328→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("agent run event error: %w", err)
   329→		}
   330→		eventCount++
   331→		if event != nil && event.IsFinalResponse() && event.Content != nil {
   332→			for _, part := range event.Content.Parts {
   333→				if part.Text != "" {
   334→					finalText += part.Text
   335→				}
   336→			}
   337→		}
   338→	}
   339→	slog.Info("agent: LLM run finished",
   340→		"run_id", run.ID,
   341→		"event_count", eventCount,
   342→		"output_length", len(finalText),
   343→	)
   344→
   345→	if finalText == "" {
   346→		slog.Error("agent: produced no output",
   347→			"run_id", run.ID,
   348→			"project_id", run.ProjectID,
   349→			"event_count", eventCount,
   350→		)
   351→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("agent produced no output")
   352→	}
   353→
   354→	// Parse the agent's response into a SemanticReport.
   355→	report, err := parseReport(finalText)
   356→	if err != nil {
   357→		slog.Warn("agent output was not valid JSON report, storing raw",
   358→			"run_id", run.ID, "parse_err", err, "raw_output", finalText)
   359→		// Fall back to storing the raw text as the summary.
   360→		report = &models.SemanticReport{
   361→			Summary: finalText,
   362→		}
   363→	}
   364→
   365→	reportJSON, err := json.Marshal(report)
   366→	if err != nil {
   367→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("marshal report: %w", err)
   368→	}
   369→
   370→	// Gather release IDs for the semantic_release_sources join table.
   371→	// Only link releases that match the target version.
   372→	releases, _, err := o.store.ListReleasesByProject(ctx, run.ProjectID, 1, 200)
   373→	if err != nil {
   374→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("list releases for semantic release: %w", err)
   375→	}
   376→	releaseIDs := make([]string, 0, len(releases))
   377→	for _, r := range releases {
   378→		if r.Version == version {
   379→			releaseIDs = append(releaseIDs, r.ID)
   380→		}
   381→	}
   382→
   383→	// Use the target version for the semantic release.
   384→	srVersion := version
   385→	if srVersion == "" {
   386→		srVersion = "unknown"
   387→		if len(releases) > 0 {
   388→			srVersion = releases[0].Version
   389→		}
   390→	}
   391→
   392→	now := time.Now()
   393→	sr := &models.SemanticRelease{
   394→		ProjectID:   run.ProjectID,
   395→		Version:     srVersion,
   396→		Report:      reportJSON,
   397→		Status:      "completed",
   398→		CompletedAt: &now,
   399→	}
   400→
   401→	if err := o.store.CreateSemanticRelease(ctx, sr, releaseIDs); err != nil {
   402→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("create semantic release: %w", err)
   403→	}
   404→
   405→	slog.Info("agent run produced semantic release",
   406→		"run_id", run.ID,
   407→		"semantic_release_id", sr.ID,
   408→		"version", sr.Version,
   409→	)
   410→
   411→	return &agentResult{
   412→		semanticReleaseID: sr.ID,
   413→		version:           srVersion,
   414→		reportText:        finalText,
   415→		projectName:       project.Name,
   416→	}, nil
   417→}
   418→
   419→// defaultSenders returns a map of channel type to Sender for the supported
   420→// notification channel types (webhook, slack, discord).
   421→func defaultSenders() map[string]routing.Sender {
   422→	httpClient := &http.Client{Timeout: 10 * time.Second}
   423→	return map[string]routing.Sender{
   424→		"webhook": &routing.WebhookSender{Client: httpClient},
   425→		"slack":   &routing.SlackSender{Client: httpClient},
   426→		"discord": &routing.DiscordSender{Client: httpClient},
   427→	}
   428→}
   429→
   430→// sendProjectNotifications looks up project-level subscriptions and sends a
   431→// notification to each channel with the semantic release report. This is
   432→// best-effort: errors are logged but do not propagate to the caller.
   433→func (o *Orchestrator) sendProjectNotifications(ctx context.Context, run *models.AgentRun, result *agentResult) {
   434→	subs, err := o.store.ListProjectSubscriptions(ctx, run.ProjectID)
   435→	if err != nil {
   436→		slog.Error("list project subscriptions for notification",
   437→			"project_id", run.ProjectID, "err", err)
   438→		return
   439→	}
   440→	if len(subs) == 0 {
   441→		slog.Debug("agent: no project subscriptions configured",
   442→			"project_id", run.ProjectID)
   443→		return
   444→	}
   445→
   446→	senders := defaultSenders()
   447→
   448→	msg := routing.Notification{
   449→		Title:   fmt.Sprintf("Semantic Release Report: %s %s", result.projectName, result.version),
   450→		Body:    result.reportText,
   451→		Version: result.version,
   452→	}
   453→
   454→	for _, sub := range subs {
   455→		ch, err := o.store.GetChannel(ctx, sub.ChannelID)
   456→		if err != nil {
   457→			slog.Error("get channel for project notification",
   458→				"channel_id", sub.ChannelID, "err", err)
   459→			continue
   460→		}
   461→
   462→		sender, ok := senders[ch.Type]
   463→		if !ok {
   464→			slog.Warn("unknown channel type for project notification", "type", ch.Type)
   465→			continue
   466→		}
   467→
   468→		if err := sender.Send(ctx, ch, msg); err != nil {
   469→			slog.Error("send project notification failed",
   470→				"channel", ch.Name, "type", ch.Type, "err", err)
   471→		} else {
   472→			slog.Info("project notification sent",
   473→				"channel", ch.Name, "project_id", run.ProjectID, "version", result.version)
   474→		}
   475→	}
   476→}
   477→
   478→// parseReport attempts to parse the agent's text output as a SemanticReport JSON.
   479→// It handles cases where the agent wraps the JSON in markdown code blocks.
   480→func parseReport(text string) (*models.SemanticReport, error) {
   481→	// Strip markdown code fences if present.
   482→	cleaned := strings.TrimSpace(text)
   483→	if strings.HasPrefix(cleaned, "```") {
   484→		// Remove opening fence (with optional language tag).
   485→		if idx := strings.Index(cleaned, "\n"); idx != -1 {
   486→			cleaned = cleaned[idx+1:]
   487→		}
   488→		// Remove closing fence.
   489→		if idx := strings.LastIndex(cleaned, "```"); idx != -1 {
   490→			cleaned = cleaned[:idx]
   491→		}
   492→		cleaned = strings.TrimSpace(cleaned)
   493→	}
   494→
   495→	var report models.SemanticReport
   496→	if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(cleaned), &report); err != nil {
   497→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("parse report JSON: %w", err)
   498→	}
   499→
   500→	if report.Subject == "" && report.Summary == "" {
   501→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("report is missing required 'subject' or 'summary' field")
   502→	}
   503→
   504→	return &report, nil
   505→}
